skill.name: vercel-deploy
skill.description: Guides deployment of Next.js applications to Vercel, including vercel.json setup, environment variables, custom domains, API rewrites, build tuning, and troubleshooting.
skill.summary: Deploy and troubleshoot Next.js applications on Vercel with practical configuration guidance.
skill.author: Awais68

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported critical risk because the markdown contains deployment commands, network examples, environment variable examples, and a helper verification script. Review found no prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent, but the skill should carry a warning because copied examples can forward Authorization headers, manipulate environment files, and run Vercel CLI commands.

medium_findings.title: Authorization Header Forwarding in API Proxy Example
medium_findings.description: The Edge Function example forwards the incoming Authorization header to an external API URL built from an environment variable. This is a legitimate proxy pattern, but copied without allowlist checks it could expose user tokens to the wrong backend.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code block directly forwards request.headers.get('Authorization') into a fetch call. The example is instructional rather than malicious, so the risk is contextual and not a block.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Environment and Deployment Token Handling
medium_findings.description: The skill documents server-only secrets, database URLs, Redis URLs, Vercel token usage, and pulling environment variables into .env.local. These are expected for deployment guidance, but mistakes can leak production credentials.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines show explicit secret-like variable names and Vercel token usage. They are placeholders and normal deployment instructions, so the risk depends on user handling.

medium_findings.title: External Deployment Commands Require User Consent
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to install the Vercel CLI, link projects, deploy to preview or production, and run builds. These are legitimate actions, but they can alter remote deployment state and should be reviewed before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clear deployment operations and package installs. They match the skill purpose, but production deploy commands have real operational impact.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged regex and markdown text as weak cryptography, but scripts/verify.py only parses frontmatter and validates names. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or custom cryptographic use in the cited code context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited script lines are regular expressions and string checks, not cryptographic functions. The markdown lines are configuration examples, not executable crypto code.

low_findings.title: Dynamic Import Alert Is Documentation Type Annotation
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged a dynamic import pattern, but the line is a JSDoc NextConfig type annotation inside a markdown code example. It is not active runtime code in the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is inside a fenced code block and uses import only as a TypeScript type reference. No executable dynamic module loading occurs in the skill.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs and IP Address Are Deployment Templates
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs and the Vercel DNS IP are used as placeholders or documented Vercel configuration values. They do not indicate exfiltration, but users should replace placeholder domains with trusted endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The values are examples such as localhost, yourdomain.com, Vercel DNS, and Vercel button URLs. I did not find evidence that the skill sends data to an unauthorized endpoint.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Alerts Are Benign Validation and Troubleshooting Text
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged system and network reconnaissance, but the cited contexts are name validation, image hostname examples, and troubleshooting advice. No evidence found of host scanning, port scanning, or system inventory collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The verifier only checks a skill name format, and the markdown text is static documentation. There is no loop, socket usage, or system enumeration logic.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential-Bearing Proxy Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The API proxy example forwards an Authorization header to a configured external API. Users should validate the destination and avoid forwarding credentials to untrusted origins.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The header forwarding is visible in the example. It is a common proxy pattern, but it is security-sensitive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Public Environment Variable Controls API Destination
dangerous_patterns.description: The rewrite and fetch examples use NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL to construct API destinations. Public configuration can be appropriate, but incorrect values can route requests to unintended services.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The destination is derived from a public environment variable in copied example code. The pattern is legitimate, but it needs deployment-time validation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Wildcard CORS Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The vercel.json header example sets Access-Control-Allow-Origin to a wildcard for API routes. This can be acceptable for public APIs, but unsafe for credentialed or private APIs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The wildcard origin is explicit in the configuration example. The risk depends on whether users apply it to endpoints that handle credentials.

content.value_statement: Deploying a Next.js app to Vercel often requires precise build, domain, API, and environment settings. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code focused guidance for production-ready Vercel deployment work.
actual_capabilities: Creates and explains vercel.json settings for Next.js projects.
Guides Vercel CLI login, project linking, preview deploys, and production deploys.
Documents environment variable setup for browser-safe and server-only values.
Provides custom domain, DNS, redirect, and HTTPS checklist guidance.
Shows API rewrite and proxy patterns for frontend-to-backend routing.
Helps troubleshoot build failures, missing environment variables, and production 404 errors.
limitations: It targets Next.js applications on Vercel, not general backend hosting.
It provides examples and checklists, but it cannot verify your Vercel account permissions.
It does not replace review of secrets, tokens, or production deployment settings.
Some code snippets must be adapted before use in credentialed APIs.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Launch Deployment
use_cases.description: Configure build commands, output settings, environment variables, and production deploy steps for a Next.js app.

use_cases.title: Connect a Custom Domain
use_cases.description: Set up domain records, HTTPS, redirects, and deployment documentation before public release.

use_cases.title: Debug Vercel Production Issues
use_cases.description: Review build failures, incorrect API URLs, missing variables, and App Router 404 behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Deployment Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me deploy my Next.js app to Vercel. Review my build command, output directory, and required environment variables.

prompt_templates.title: Environment Variable Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Vercel environment variable plan. Separate browser-safe variables from server-only secrets and identify risky names.

prompt_templates.title: Domain and Rewrite Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Design my Vercel custom domain, redirects, DNS records, and API rewrites for a Next.js frontend with a separate backend.

prompt_templates.title: Production Deployment Audit
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit my Next.js Vercel deployment plan for build reliability, security headers, secret exposure, API routing, and rollback risks.

output_examples.input: Deploy my Next.js app with a custom domain and API backend.
output: A deployment checklist covering build, environment variables, DNS records, redirects, HTTPS, and API routing.
A warning to avoid exposing server-only secrets with browser-visible prefixes.

output_examples.input: My Vercel production build cannot reach the API.
output: A troubleshooting path that checks environment names, production values, rewrite destinations, redeploy timing, and backend availability.
A recommendation to confirm the API URL points to the correct environment.
A launch readiness review for build success, domain setup, cache headers, custom error pages, and secret handling.
A list of items to verify manually in the Vercel dashboard.

best_practices: Review every production deploy command before running it.
Keep server-only secrets out of NEXT_PUBLIC variables.
Validate API rewrite destinations before forwarding authorization headers.
anti_patterns: Do not copy placeholder domains into production without replacing them.
Do not use wildcard CORS on private or credentialed API routes.
Do not pull production secrets into local files without access controls.

faq.question: Does this skill deploy the app automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides commands and configuration guidance that a user or agent may execute after review.

faq.question: What framework does it support best?
faq.answer: It is focused on Next.js applications deployed to Vercel.

faq.question: Can it help with custom domains?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes DNS records, redirect guidance, and Vercel dashboard steps.

faq.question: Does it handle secrets safely?
faq.answer: It explains browser-visible and server-only variables, but users must protect real secrets.

faq.question: Can it troubleshoot failed builds?
faq.answer: Yes. It gives checks for local builds, Vercel builds, environment variables, and routing issues.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium-risk warning because it includes deployment commands and credential-sensitive examples.
